sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: After 40 minutes of dark adaptation, the visual threshold is lowest at which wavelength?
w: Full dark adaptation is scotopic (rod-driven); rods peak at ~507 nm. 555 nm is the photopic cone peak.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: How long does full, rod-based dark adaptation take?
w: Cones plateau at ~7 min (Kohlrausch kink); rhodopsin regenerates slowly, so full rod adaptation takes 30–40 min.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: The inflection point at ~7 minutes on the dark adaptation curve, where rods take over from cones, is the:
w: The Kohlrausch kink marks the cone-to-rod transition at about 7 minutes.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: On Hirschberg the corneal reflex sits temporal in both eyes, but cover test shows no movement. The cause is:
w: Positive kappa puts the pupil nasal to the visual axis, so the reflex looks temporal, mimicking exotropia. Negative cover test confirms pseudo-exo.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: A nasally displaced corneal light reflex with a negative cover test indicates:
w: Negative kappa puts the pupil temporal, so the reflex sits nasal, simulating esotropia (pseudo-eso).

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Rhodopsin fails to regenerate after light exposure. Which step is impaired?
o: Re-isomerization of retinal in the RPE
Cone pigment synthesis in the fovea
Glutamate release in the dark current
w: The visual cycle regenerates 11-cis retinal from all-trans retinal in the RPE; blocking it stops rhodopsin regeneration.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Which is true of the fovea?
o: Equal numbers of rods and cones
w: The fovea excludes rods entirely and holds the highest cone density (~150,000/mm²) for peak acuity.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Why is a dim star easier to see by looking slightly to the side of it?
o: Rods peak at ~20° from the fovea
w: Averted vision lands the image on peripheral retina where rod density peaks (~20°), maximizing scotopic sensitivity.
k: Averted vision targets peak rod zone (~20°).

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: In the axes of Fick, the anteroposterior (Y) axis, 24 mm long, controls which movement?
w: The AP axis is longest (24 mm) and controls torsion. X = vertical, Z = horizontal.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: The vertical (Z) axis controls which eye movement?
w: Rotation about the vertical axis produces horizontal movements. The X (transverse) axis produces vertical movements.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Angle alpha is measured at which point?
w: Angle alpha (optical vs visual axis) is measured at the nodal point; angle kappa is measured at the pupil.
k: Alpha at nodal point; kappa at pupil.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: At dusk a blue flower looks brighter than a red one that seemed equal at noon. This is the:
w: As light fades, sensitivity shifts from the photopic 555 nm peak to the scotopic 507 nm (blue-green) peak, so blues relatively brighten.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Ricco's law describes which kind of summation?
w: Ricco's law: within the critical area, area × intensity is constant (spatial). Bunsen-Roscoe covers temporal summation.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: The law stating that time × intensity is constant for a brief stimulus (temporal summation) is:
w: Bunsen-Roscoe governs temporal summation (up to ~100 ms for rods).

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Which cone pigment is most sensitive to long-wavelength (red) light?
w: Erythrolabe (L-cones, ~750 nm) = red; chlorolabe (M, 535 nm) = green; cyanolabe (S, 440 nm) = blue.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: All three axes of eye rotation pass through the center of rotation, located approximately:
w: The center of rotation is ~13.5 mm behind the cornea; all axes of Fick pass through it.
k: Center of rotation 13.5 mm behind cornea.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Compared with the cone plateau, rods at full dark adaptation are about:
w: Dark adaptation improves threshold by ~3 log units, making rods roughly 1000× more sensitive than cones.

sec: B1 Visual System and Ocular Optics
q: Which lowers the visual threshold (increases sensitivity)?
w: Binocular viewing combines neural signals from both eyes, lowering threshold. A larger pupil also helps.
k: Two eyes beat one for threshold.

q: In early optic neuritis, which visual function is affected earliest?
w: Color is the most vulnerable function (basis of the red-desaturation test); motion is the most resilient.

q: Which visual function is the most resilient, lost last in optic nerve disease?
w: Motion perception survives longest; color is lost first.

q: Which LGB (lateral geniculate body) layers receive contralateral (crossed) input?
w: Layers 1, 4, 6 are contralateral (nasal/crossed); layers 2, 3, 5 are ipsilateral (temporal/uncrossed).

q: The primary visual cortex (area 17) is mainly responsible for:
w: Area 17 detects basic features. Object recognition needs the ventral stream through areas 18, 19, and inferotemporal cortex.
k: Area 17 = basic features, not object ID.

q: The ventral visual stream is responsible for:
w: Ventral ('what') runs to the temporal lobe; dorsal ('where/how') runs to the parietal lobe.

q: The dorsal visual stream processes:
w: The dorsal stream handles 'where/how' (spatial and motion) and projects to the parietal lobe.

q: Background intensity doubles from 100 to 200 units. By Weber's law, the just-noticeable difference (JND):
w: Weber's law: ΔI/I = k. The ratio is constant, so when I doubles, the absolute JND doubles.

q: The red-desaturation test (a red target looks washed out in one eye) detects:
w: Because color conduction is the most vulnerable, red desaturation flags early optic nerve compromise.
k: Red desaturation = early optic nerve disease.

q: Area 18 of the visual cortex is best described as:
w: Area 17 = primary striate; area 18 = parastriate (secondary); area 19 = peristriate (higher).

q: A lesion isolated to area 17 of one hemisphere would most directly impair:
w: Area 17 is primary visual cortex; damage causes a contralateral field defect of basic vision.

q: Object recognition (the 'what' pathway) requires processing beyond area 17 through:
o: The dorsal stream to parietal lobe
The ventral stream to IT cortex
w: Recognition is a ventral-stream function extending to inferotemporal cortex; area 17 alone only detects features.
k: Recognition = ventral stream to IT cortex.

q: Which LGB layers carry ipsilateral (uncrossed, temporal retina) input?
w: Layers 2, 3, 5 are ipsilateral; 1, 4, 6 are contralateral.

q: Weber's law is the physiological basis of which clinical measurement?
w: Because the JND scales with background intensity, contrast detection (ΔI/I) underlies contrast sensitivity testing.

q: The normal temporal extent of the monocular visual field is:
w: Temporal is widest at 100°. Superior 60°, inferior 75°, nasal 60°.

q: The normal nasal and superior extents of the visual field are about:
w: Nasal and superior are each ~60°; inferior 75°; temporal 100°.

q: The inferior extent of the normal visual field is approximately:
w: Inferior is ~75°, between nasal/superior (60°) and temporal (100°).

q: How much of the visual field is seen binocularly?
w: The central 120° overlaps binocularly; each side has a 30° monocular temporal crescent.

q: The monocular temporal crescent (seen by one eye only) is about:
w: Beyond the 120° binocular zone, each eye sees a 30° temporal crescent alone.

q: On Goldmann perimetry, which stimulus size is the largest?
w: Size V (64 mm²) is largest, used to map peripheral field in RP and low vision; size I is smallest.

q: The smallest Goldmann stimulus is:
w: Size I (0.25 mm²) is the smallest; size V (64 mm²) is the largest.

q: The standard Goldmann perimeter background luminance is:
w: Goldmann uses a 31.5 apostilb background in a bowl of 300 mm radius.

q: The Amsler grid tests which portion of the visual field?
w: Each square is 1°; the grid covers only the central 10°, used for macular disease (metamorphopsia).

q: A bitemporal hemianopia localizes the lesion to the:
w: Crossing nasal fibers at the chiasm are damaged, producing bitemporal hemianopia, classically from a pituitary adenoma.

q: A superior quadrantanopia ('pie in the sky') localizes to:
w: Inferior retinal fibers loop through the temporal lobe (Meyer's loop); damage gives a contralateral superior quadrant defect.
k: Pie in the sky = Meyer's loop (temporal).

q: Severely constricted fields with a small central island, plus years of night blindness, suggest:
w: RP is a rod dystrophy: a ring scotoma expands to leave a central island, with early nyctalopia.

q: Within the visual field, which function is the most vulnerable (lost first)?
w: Color is the most vulnerable; motion is the most resilient.
k: Color most vulnerable, motion most resilient.

q: The most common color vision deficiency is:
w: Deuteranomaly (weak green) is most common; tritan defects are rare and autosomal.

q: Additive mixing of red, green, and blue light produces:
w: Additive (light) mixing R+G+B = white; subtractive (pigment) mixing C+M+Y = black.

q: Subtractive mixing of cyan, magenta, and yellow pigments produces:
w: Subtractive pigment mixing C+M+Y = black; additive light mixing gives white.

q: What is the purpose of Ishihara plate 1 (the '12' plate)?
w: Plate 1 is the control: everyone, including color-deficient patients, reads it. It confirms task understanding.

q: To precisely classify the axis and severity of a color defect, the best test is:
w: The Farnsworth-Munsell 100 Hue test plots a total error score and the exact axis of confusion. Ishihara only screens red-green.

q: The trichromatic theory of color (at the receptor level) is credited to:
w: Young-Helmholtz = trichromatic (three cones, receptor level); Hering = opponent process (post-receptor).

q: The opponent-process theory of color (post-receptor level) is credited to:
w: Hering proposed opponent channels (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white) at the post-receptor level.

q: The S-cone pigment cyanolabe is most sensitive at about:
w: Cyanolabe (S-cone) peaks ~440 nm (blue); chlorolabe ~535 nm (green); erythrolabe ~750 nm (red).

q: In protanopia, red light appears:
w: Without L-cones, red light poorly stimulates the remaining cones, so reds look dim.

q: How does the HRR test differ from Ishihara?
w: HRR (using symbols) screens both red-green and blue-yellow; Ishihara screens red-green only.

q: The FALANT (Farnsworth Lantern) is primarily a(n):
w: FALANT is an occupational lantern test (e.g., for navigation/aviation), performed at 8 feet, not a diagnostic axis test.

q: The prevalence of red-green color deficiency in males is approximately:
w: Red-green deficiency affects ~8% of males (X-linked), versus ~0.5% of females.

q: Tritan (blue-yellow) congenital color defects are:
w: Congenital tritanopia is rare and autosomal, unlike the common X-linked red-green defects.

q: What is the normal AC/A ratio?
w: Normal AC/A is 4:1 to 6:1. High AC/A drives excess conditions; low AC/A drives insufficiency.

q: A high AC/A ratio is associated with which conditions?
w: High AC/A = too much convergence per diopter = excess conditions (CE, DE).

q: Convergence insufficiency is characterized by:
o: Low AC/A, exo at near, receded NPC
w: CI has a low AC/A, exophoria greater at near, and a receded NPC, with near symptoms.
k: CI = low AC/A, near exo, receded NPC.

q: Which is the slowest eye movement system?
w: Vergence is slowest (~20°/s). Saccades are fastest (500–1000°/s); VOR has the shortest latency.

q: Which eye movement has the shortest (fastest) latency?
w: The VOR has ~15 ms latency, the fastest reflex, stabilizing gaze during head movement.

q: Saccadic velocity is approximately:
w: Saccades are the fastest voluntary movement at 500–1000°/s; vergence is the slowest.

q: Sheard's criterion for comfortable fusion requires the fusional reserve to be at least:
w: Sheard's: compensating fusional reserve ≥ 2× the phoria. An 8 PD exophoria needs ≥16 PD PFC.

q: Percival's criterion states that the phoria should fall within:
o: The first third of the range
The middle third of the fusional range
w: Percival's: the demand line (phoria) should sit in the middle third of the total fusional vergence range.

q: In Worth's classification, simultaneous macular perception is:
w: Grade 1 = simultaneous perception, Grade 2 = flat fusion, Grade 3 = stereopsis.

q: In Worth's classification, flat (superimposition) fusion with amplitude is:
w: Grade 2 is fusion (merging two images, with vergence amplitude). Grade 3 is stereopsis.

q: Hering's law of equal innervation applies to:
w: Hering's law governs binocular yoke muscles (equal innervation). Sherrington's is the monocular agonist-antagonist rule.

q: Sherrington's law of reciprocal innervation describes:
w: Sherrington's law is monocular: when the agonist contracts, its antagonist relaxes.

q: An object beyond (farther than) the fixation point produces what physiologic diplopia?
w: Far objects stimulate nasal retina, giving uncrossed (homonymous) diplopia; near objects give crossed.

q: An object closer than the fixation point produces:
w: Near objects stimulate temporal retina, producing crossed (heteronymous) diplopia.

q: Normal retinal correspondence (NRC) is maintained in:
w: In heterophoria, binocular vision is kept most of the time, preserving NRC. Constant strabismus may develop ARC.

q: Anomalous retinal correspondence (ARC) typically develops in:
w: A fixed, constant deviation gives the brain time to rewire correspondence (ARC) to avoid diplopia.

q: In harmonious ARC, the anomalous neutral point:
o: Is less than the angle of deviation
w: Harmonious ARC is fully adapted: the anomalous angle equals the objective angle of deviation.

q: The normal near point of convergence (NPC) break is:
w: Normal NPC break is ≤8 cm; greater than 10 cm suggests convergence insufficiency.

q: Panum's fusional area at the fovea is about:
w: Panum's area is very small at the fovea (6–10 arc min) and broadens peripherally.

q: A hyperope who is straight at distance but crosses at near, with a high AC/A, has:
w: Accommodative esotropia: hyperopic accommodation drives excess convergence (high AC/A), crossing the eyes at near.

q: For an exophoria, vision therapy and prism approaches aim to:
w: Exophoria is managed by building fusional convergence (convergence exercises) or temporary base-out prism. Esophoria uses base-in/plus.

q: Which amblyopia type generally has the worst prognosis?
w: Deprivation amblyopia (cataract, ptosis, opacity) has the worst prognosis and must be treated within the critical period.

q: Divergence excess shows which pattern?
o: Equal exo at near and distance
Exo greater at distance, high AC/A
Eso at distance, ortho at near
Receded NPC with exo at near
w: Divergence excess: large exo at distance, near closer to ortho, with a high AC/A.
k: DE = exo far, ortho near, high AC/A.

q: The most common type of amblyopia is:
w: Strabismic amblyopia (suppression of a constantly deviating eye) is the most common type.

q: What is normal stereoacuity?
w: Normal stereoacuity is 20–40 seconds of arc; smaller arc-second values mean finer stereopsis.

q: Which stereoacuity value represents the FINEST depth perception?
w: Smaller arc-second values are finer; 20 sec arc is far better than 400 sec arc.

q: Stereopsis corresponds to which Worth grade of binocular vision?
w: Grade 3 (stereopsis) is the highest; Grade 2 is flat fusion, Grade 1 is simultaneous perception.

q: A patient with deep constant strabismic amblyopia (20/200) is tested on the Randot. Expected result:
w: Deep suppression and disrupted binocular neurons abolish stereopsis regardless of the dominant eye's acuity.

q: Which stereopsis test uses real (physical) depth and requires no special glasses?
w: The Frisby uses transparent plates of real thickness, so no polarized/red-green glasses are needed.

q: On the Titmus test, the 'fly' represents which (coarsest) disparity?
w: The Titmus fly is a gross stereo target at ~3000 sec arc, used to confirm any gross stereopsis.

q: Stereopsis normally first develops at about:
w: Binocular cortical neurons mature so stereopsis emerges around 3–6 months; it is not present at birth.

q: A patient who loses one eye retains depth perception from:
w: A monocular patient loses stereopsis but keeps monocular cues and adapts well for daily function.
k: Monocular cues survive loss of stereopsis.

q: Stereopsis specifically depends on:
w: Stereopsis arises from small horizontal disparities between the two retinal images within Panum's area.

q: Stereopsis requires which prerequisites?
w: Stereopsis (Grade 3) is built on simultaneous perception (Grade 1) and fusion (Grade 2).

q: Convergence as a depth cue provides useful information only within about:
w: Convergence is a useful depth cue only at near (within ~2 m); beyond that, disparity and monocular cues dominate.

q: A 7-year-old with 20/20 acuity and normal BV cannot find objects on a busy worksheet and loses his place reading. Best assessment:
w: These figure-ground and sequencing problems are visual perceptual (VPS). With acuity and BV normal, TVPS-3 (pointing-only) is appropriate.

q: A patient has 20/20 acuity but struggles with visual processing tasks. This shows that:
o: 20/20 acuity does NOT rule out a VIP deficit
w: Visual information processing is separate from acuity; 20/20 does not exclude a VIP/VPS deficit.
k: 20/20 does not rule out VIP deficit.

q: How do VIP and VPS relate?
o: VPS includes motor output; VIP does not
VIP is broad; VPS is the non-motor subset
w: Visual information processing (VIP) is the broad domain; visual perceptual skills (VPS) are its non-motor subset.

q: The Beery VMI primarily measures:
w: Beery VMI requires copying shapes, testing visual-motor integration; a low score may be perceptual OR motor.

q: The TVPS-3 is best described as:
w: TVPS-3 uses pointing responses to assess all seven non-motor VPS subtopics in ages 4–18.

q: A child scores poorly on the Beery VMI. To separate perception from motor output, do which test next?
w: TVPS-3 needs no motor output; if normal, the Beery deficit is motor; if poor, it is perceptual.
k: Use TVPS-3 to isolate perception from motor.

q: The DTVP-2 is notable because it:
w: The DTVP-2 yields both motor-reduced and motor-enhanced scores, helping localize the deficit, in ages 4–10.

q: Difficulty recognizing a shape when its size or orientation changes is a deficit of:
w: Form constancy is recognizing an object despite changes in size, orientation, or context.
k: Form constancy = same shape, changed size/orientation.

q: Identifying a complete object from an incomplete or partial drawing is the skill of:
w: Visual closure is recognizing a whole from fragmentary visual information.
k: Visual closure = whole from incomplete figure.

q: Trouble locating a target object within a cluttered background is a deficit of:
w: Figure-ground is separating the relevant object from a busy background; failing it causes 'lost on the page' behavior.

q: Which assessment is most appropriate when handwriting and shape-copying are the chief complaints?
w: Copying and handwriting are visual-motor tasks, best evaluated by the Beery VMI.

q: Cone-mediated dark adaptation is essentially complete by about:
w: Cones finish adapting at roughly 7 minutes, producing the Kohlrausch kink where cones plateau and rods take over. Full rod adaptation needs 30–40 minutes.

q: The Kohlrausch kink on the dark adaptation curve marks:
o: The handover from cones to rods
The onset of the Purkinje shift
w: The kink at about 7 minutes is the break in the curve where cones have plateaued and the slower, more sensitive rods take over.

q: The Purkinje shift describes the peak sensitivity moving:
w: Going from photopic to scotopic conditions, peak sensitivity shifts from 555 nm (cones, yellow-green) to 507 nm (rods, blue-green). This is why reds look black at dusk while blues stay visible.

q: Retinal pH in the dark compared with the light is:
w: The dark retina sits at about pH 7.3 (alkaline); in light it falls to roughly 7.0 (acidic).

q: Angle alpha is the angle between the optical and visual axes measured at the:
w: Angle alpha is subtended at the nodal point and averages about 5° temporal. A large positive angle kappa mimics exotropia.
k: Angle alpha = at the nodal point, ~5° temporal.

q: A positive angle kappa produces the appearance of:
w: With a positive kappa the corneal reflex sits nasally, making the eye look turned out. It is an artifact of reflex position, not a real deviation.

q: Ricco's law of spatial summation states that:
w: Ricco's law covers spatial summation: within the summation area, threshold depends on the product of area and intensity. Bunsen-Roscoe is the temporal analogue, where time × intensity is constant.
k: Ricco = area × intensity; Bunsen-Roscoe = time × intensity.

q: The red-sensitive cone pigment is called:
w: Erythrolabe is the long-wavelength (red) pigment, chlorolabe the middle (green), and cyanolabe the short (blue).
k: Erythrolabe R, chlorolabe G, cyanolabe B.

q: The longest axis of the globe, the anteroposterior axis, measures about:
w: The AP (Y) axis is about 24 mm and is the longest; the vertical and horizontal axes run about 22 mm each.

q: Which LGB layers receive crossed (nasal) fibers?
w: Layers 1, 4 and 6 take contralateral crossed nasal fibers; layers 2, 3 and 5 take ipsilateral uncrossed temporal fibers.

q: Brodmann area 18 is known as the:
w: Area 17 is striate (primary), 18 is parastriate (secondary), and 19 is peristriate (higher-order).

q: Which visual function fails earliest in optic nerve disease?
w: Color is the most vulnerable function and drops out first, which is why red desaturation is an early optic neuritis sign. Motion is the most resilient and goes last.
k: Color fails first; motion survives longest.

q: The dorsal stream to the parietal lobe processes:
w: The dorsal 'where/how' stream handles spatial relationships and motion; the ventral 'what' stream in the temporal lobe handles object recognition.

q: The normal temporal extent of the monocular visual field is about:
w: Normal extents run superior 60°, inferior 75°, temporal 100°, nasal 60°. The temporal field is widest because the nose blocks nothing on that side.

q: The monocular temporal crescent measures roughly:
w: The temporal crescent is the outer 30° seen by one eye alone, beyond the 120° of binocular overlap.

q: Which Goldmann stimulus size is used to map the far periphery in advanced field loss?
w: Size V is the largest stimulus at 64 mm². The V4e isopter maps residual peripheral field in retinitis pigmentosa and low vision. Size I is smallest at 0.25 mm².
k: V = largest (64 mm²); I = smallest (0.25 mm²).

q: On the Amsler grid, one small square subtends:
w: Each square equals 1° of visual field, and the whole grid covers the central 10° at the standard 30 cm working distance.

q: A 'pie in the sky' superior quadrantanopia localizes to:
w: Meyer's loop carries inferior retinal fibers through the temporal lobe, so a lesion there drops the contralateral superior quadrant: pie in the sky.
k: Pie in the sky = Meyer's loop, temporal lobe.

q: The trichromatic theory of Young-Helmholtz explains color coding at the level of the:
w: Young-Helmholtz trichromacy operates at the receptor level with three cone types. Hering's opponent process explains post-receptor coding, and stage theory reconciles the two.

q: The opponent process theory of color vision was proposed by:
w: Hering's opponent process describes red-green, blue-yellow and black-white channels beyond the receptors, explaining afterimages and why no color looks reddish-green.

q: The most common inherited color vision defect is:
w: Deuteranomaly, a green weakness rather than a total loss, is the commonest defect. Red-green deficiency overall affects about 8% of males.

q: The first plate of the Ishihara test shows '12' because it is:
o: A control plate everyone can read
w: Plate 1 is a demonstration plate visible to all observers, including total color defectives. Failing it suggests malingering or very poor acuity rather than a color defect.

q: The most comprehensive clinical test of color discrimination is the:
w: The Farnsworth-Munsell 100 Hue arranges caps by subtle hue steps and grades severity as well as type. Ishihara only screens red-green.

q: The FALANT occupational color test is performed at:
w: The Farnsworth Lantern is run at 8 feet and simulates signal-light recognition for maritime and aviation screening.

q: The normal AC/A ratio is about:
w: Normal AC/A runs 4:1 to 6:1. It is high in convergence excess and divergence excess, and low in convergence and divergence insufficiency.

q: The slowest of the eye movements is:
w: Vergence is slowest at about 20°/s. The VOR is fastest with a 15 ms latency; saccades have a 200 ms latency.

q: Sheard's criterion requires the fusional reserve to be at least:
w: Sheard's criterion holds that the opposing fusional reserve should be at least twice the phoria for comfortable single vision. Failing it supports prescribing prism or starting therapy.

q: Worth Grade 1 of binocular vision is:
w: Grade 1 is simultaneous perception, Grade 2 flat fusion with amplitude, Grade 3 stereopsis. Grades 1 and 2 are prerequisites for Grade 3.

q: Physiologic diplopia for an object beyond the fixation point is:
w: Objects farther than fixation give uncrossed physiologic diplopia; objects nearer than fixation give crossed diplopia. It is a normal finding and a useful anti-suppression cue.

q: The normal near point of convergence is:
w: A normal NPC breaks at 8 cm or closer. Beyond 10 cm suggests convergence insufficiency.

q: Normal stereoacuity is about:
w: Normal stereoacuity is 20–40 arc seconds. The Randot resolves to about 20 arc seconds; the Titmus fly is the coarsest target at 3000 arc seconds.

q: Panum's fusional area at the fovea spans about:
w: Panum's area is the zone of disparity within which two retinal images still fuse into one. It is narrowest at the fovea (6–10 arc min) and widens peripherally.

q: The coarsest target on the Titmus stereotest is the:
w: The Titmus fly is 3000 arc seconds, gross enough that a patient with no stereopsis may still pass it by monocular cues, so it is a screen rather than a measure.

q: The relationship between VIP and VPS is that:
o: VPS is the non-motor subset of VIP
VIP is a subset of VPS
w: Visual information processing is the broad term covering processing plus motor output. Visual perceptual skills are the non-motor subset within it.
k: VIP broad; VPS = its non-motor subset.

q: The Beery VMI assesses a child primarily by asking them to:
w: The Beery VMI measures visual-motor integration through shape copying and spans ages 2 to 100. The TVPS-3 is deliberately non-motor and uses pointing only.

q: The TVPS-3 is designed to be motor-free by having the child:
w: The TVPS-3 requires pointing only, so a child with poor motor skills is not penalized on a perceptual test. It covers ages 4 to 18.

q: Horizontal eye movements (adduction and abduction) occur around which axis?
w: The Z (vertical) axis governs horizontal movement, the X axis governs vertical movement, and the Y (anteroposterior) axis governs torsion. The axis is named for the line the eye rotates around, not the direction it moves.
k: Z = horizontal, X = vertical, Y = torsion.

q: Angle kappa, unlike angle alpha, is measured at the:
w: Angle kappa is subtended at the pupil; angle alpha at the nodal point. Both describe the offset between the optical and visual axes, but at different reference points.
k: Kappa at the pupil; alpha at the nodal point.

q: A positive angle kappa on Hirschberg testing is distinguished from true exotropia by:
w: Positive kappa shifts the reflex temporally, mimicking exotropia, but the cover test shows no movement because there is no true deviation. It is pseudo-exotropia.
k: Pseudo-exotropia: reflex shifted, cover test negative.

q: The 30° monocular crescent at each side of the field is seen by the:
w: The central 120° is binocular. Beyond it, each temporal retina alone sees a 30° crescent, which is why a chiasmal lesion costs those far temporal fields.
k: Monocular crescent = 30°, seen by temporal retina.

q: In protanopia, red objects appear:
w: Protanopes lack L-cones, and red light stimulates the M- and S-cones poorly, so red loses luminance as well as hue. A red traffic light can look almost black.

q: Tritanopia differs from the red-green defects in that it is:
w: Tritan defects are autosomal and rare, so they affect the sexes equally. Red-green defects are X-linked, which is why they hit about 8% of males.

q: On the FM-100 Hue test, the patient responds by:
w: The FM-100 uses hue-graded caps the patient arranges in sequence, and the error score grades severity and type. Ishihara uses numerals and HRR uses symbols.

q: The fastest VOLUNTARY eye movement is the:
w: The saccade is the fastest voluntary movement, at a 200 ms latency. The VOR is the fastest reflex at 15 ms but is not voluntary, and vergence is slowest at 20°/s.
k: Saccade fastest voluntary; VOR fastest reflex.

q: Convergence insufficiency is characterized by an AC/A ratio that is:
w: CI carries a low AC/A: accommodating produces too little convergence. High AC/A drives the excess conditions, convergence excess and divergence excess.
k: CI = low AC/A; excess conditions = high AC/A.

q: Why does a heterophoria preserve normal retinal correspondence?
o: The angle is too small to adapt
w: A phoria is compensated by fusion nearly all the time, so the eyes stay aligned and NRC persists. ARC needs the constant, unchanging misalignment of a constant strabismus.
k: Phoria keeps NRC; constant strabismus breeds ARC.

q: Suppression treated early within the critical period is:
w: Suppression itself is reversible when treated early. The danger is that prolonged suppression breeds amblyopia, which becomes permanent once the critical period closes around 7 to 8 years.
k: Suppression reversible early; untreated it becomes amblyopia.

q: During Hirschberg testing, the corneal light reflex falls approximately 1mm temporal to the corneal center in both eyes. Cover test reveals no movement. What is the most likely explanation?
w: Positive angle kappa exists when the center of the entrance pupil is nasal to the visual axis. Because the pupil is nasal, the corneal light reflex appears displaced temporally — simulating exotropia on Hirschberg. The negative cover test confirms no real deviation is present. This is pseudo-exotropia due to positive angle kappa. Negative kappa would produce a nasal reflex displacement (pseudo-esotropia). Angle alpha is measured at the nodal point and does not produce this clinical finding directly.
k: Positive angle kappa exists when the center of the entrance pupil is nasal to the visual axis.

q: A patient is tested in a dark room after 40 minutes of adaptation. At what wavelength of monochromatic light would their visual threshold be lowest?
w: After 40 minutes of dark adaptation, the visual system is operating in scotopic mode — driven entirely by rods. Rods have maximum sensitivity at approximately 507 nm (blue-green). The 555 nm peak applies to photopic (cone-driven) conditions. 440 nm is the S-cone peak (cyanolabe), relevant in photopic color vision. 535 nm is the M-cone (chlorolabe) peak. In scotopic conditions, all three cone types are essentially inactive, and rod sensitivity dominates.
k: After 40 minutes of dark adaptation, the visual system is operating in scotopic mode — driven entirely by…

q: A patient's rhodopsin cannot regenerate after light exposure. Which step in the visual cycle is most likely impaired?
o: Re-isomerization of all-trans retinal to 11-cis retinal in the RPE
Release of glutamate at the synaptic terminal in the dark
Transport of all-trans retinal from photoreceptors to RPE
Photoisomerization of 11-cis retinal in the outer segment
w: Rhodopsin regeneration depends on recycling spent chromophore (all-trans retinal) back to its usable form (11-cis retinal). This enzymatic re-isomerization occurs in the RPE, not in the photoreceptor outer segment. After light hits rhodopsin, 11-cis retinal photoisomerizes to all-trans (this is the TRIGGER, not the regeneration step). All-trans retinal exits the photoreceptor and enters the RPE where the RPE65 enzyme converts it back to 11-cis retinal. RPE65 mutations cause Leber congenital amaurosis — a genetic cause of severe visual impairment. Transport is a prerequisite for C but not the regeneration step itself. Glutamate release is the dark-current synapse activity, unrelated to regeneration.
k: Rhodopsin regeneration depends on recycling spent chromophore (all-trans retinal) back to its usable form…

q: A patient with multiple sclerosis has early optic neuritis. Which of the following visual functions would be affected earliest?
w: Color is the most vulnerable visual function — it is selectively affected early in optic nerve disease because color perception depends on intact, well-myelinated, high-frequency nerve fiber conduction. Even subtle demyelination (as in early optic neuritis) preferentially disrupts color transmission before form or motion are noticeably impaired. This is the basis of the "red desaturation" test — patients with early optic neuritis report that a bright red target appears washed-out or pink in the affected eye compared to the normal eye. Motion is the most resilient function and is typically preserved until late disease.
k: Color is the most vulnerable visual function — it is selectively affected early in optic nerve disease…

q: A researcher doubles the intensity of a light stimulus from 100 to 200 units. According to Weber's Law, what must be true about the just noticeable difference (JND)?
o: The JND stays a fixed fraction of the new background intensity
The JND doubles because the background intensity doubled
The JND stays constant regardless of the background intensity
The JND decreases because more light is available
w: Weber's Law states: ΔI/I = k. The ratio of the just noticeable difference (ΔI) to the background intensity (I) is a constant (k) — not ΔI itself. When the background doubles from 100 to 200, the new JND = k �� 200, which is twice the original JND. The absolute threshold goes up, but the ratio stays the same. This is the clinical basis for contrast sensitivity: in dim conditions (low I), a smaller ΔI is needed; in bright conditions (high I), a larger ΔI is needed for detection.

q: An ipRGC (intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cell) is stimulated by sustained bright light. Which of the following is the PRIMARY function being activated?
o: High-acuity pattern vision at the fovea
Circadian rhythm entrainment via the suprachiasmatic nucleus
Motion detection in the peripheral visual field
ON-pathway activation feeding into the LGB parvocellular layers
w: IpRGCs express melanopsin and project primarily to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus via the retinohypothalamic tract — the master circadian clock pathway. They also project to the pretectal area (pupillary light reflex) and intergeniculate leaflet. They are non-image-forming cells — they do NOT contribute to pattern vision, motion detection, or LGB parvocellular/magnocellular pathways. Their slow, sustained response to ambient light level makes them ideal for signaling time-of-day (circadian entrainment) rather than detecting rapid changes in the scene. Disrupting this system (e.g., blue-light exposure at night) suppresses melatonin and disrupts sleep cycles.
k: IpRGCs express melanopsin and project primarily to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus via…

q: On Goldmann perimetry, all isopters are severely constricted, with only a small central island of vision remaining. The patient reports difficulty seeing in dim light and has had peripheral vision loss for years. What condition does this pattern suggest?
w: Retinitis pigmentosa is a rod dystrophy that begins peripherally and progresses centrally, creating a classic "ring scotoma" that expands until only a small central island of cone vision remains. On Goldmann perimetry, this appears as severely constricted isopters around a preserved central island. The history of night blindness (rod dysfunction precedes cone loss in RP) and progressive peripheral loss over years is consistent with RP. Pituitary adenoma causes bitemporal hemianopia — not concentric constriction. Glaucoma causes arcuate scotomas and nasal steps, typically not this degree of peripheral constriction early. CSR causes a central scotoma, not peripheral.
k: Retinitis pigmentosa is a rod dystrophy that begins peripherally and progresses centrally, creating a classic…

q: A patient fails multiple Ishihara plates and is suspected of having a color vision deficiency. To determine the exact axis and severity of the defect, which test should be ordered?
w: The FM-100 Hue test is the most comprehensive color vision assessment. Using 85 hue-graded colored caps arranged in four boxes, it plots a "total error score" and identifies the exact axis of color confusion (the angle of the error vector). This allows precise classification of both type and severity of color defect. D-15 is useful for screening mild vs severe defects and type, but is less detailed. FALANT is an occupational test, not a diagnostic one. HRR screens for both red-green and blue-yellow defects but does not quantify the defect as precisely as FM-100.
k: The FM-100 Hue test is the most comprehensive color vision assessment.

q: A 12-year-old patient reports headaches and blurred vision after 20 minutes of reading. Distance acuity is 20/20 OU. Cover test at distance shows orthophoria; cover test at near reveals 12PD exophoria. NPC breaks at 14 cm and recovers at 22 cm. Positive Fusional Convergence at near is 8/10/6 (break/recovery). What is the most likely diagnosis and what does Sheard's criterion indicate?
w: The pattern — orthophoria at distance + significant exophoria at near + receded NPC (>10cm) + near symptoms — defines convergence insufficiency (low AC/A). Sheard's criterion requires positive fusional convergence ≥ 2× the near exophoria: 2 × 12PD = 24PD needed. The PFC blur/break is only 10PD — well below the 24PD required. The patient fails Sheard's, which explains the symptoms (the system is working near capacity). Management: plus adds for near to reduce accommodative convergence demand, plus vision therapy (convergence exercises — pencil push-ups, Brock string).
k: The pattern — orthophoria at distance + significant exophoria at near + receded NPC (>10cm) + near symptoms…

q: A child with right esotropia is examined with the Hering-Bielschowsky after-image test. The after-image from the right eye appears displaced to the left of the after-image from the left eye, indicating a non-symmetric result. What does this finding indicate?
w: In the Hering-Bielschowsky after-image test with NRC, the after-images would appear as a symmetric cross (equal displacement matching the angle of strabismus). Displacement in a non-symmetric pattern that exactly matches the angle of strabismus = harmonious ARC, where the anomalous neutral point has fully adapted to equal the angle of deviation. The brain has rewired — the extrafoveal area of the right eye now shares the same visual direction as the left fovea. Unharmonious ARC would show the neutral point NOT matching the deviation. Suppression would prevent the after-image from being seen at all.
k: In the Hering-Bielschowsky after-image test with NRC, the after-images would appear as a symmetric cross…

q: A patient presents with spontaneous oscillating eye movements with no fast phase. The movements are equal in both directions. He reports the movements were present since birth and improve slightly when he converges. What type of nystagmus does this describe?
o: Infantile nystagmus syndrome with null point
Jerk nystagmus from a vestibular lesion
w: The key features: (1) present since birth — congenital onset; (2) pendular in primary position (equal velocity both ways — no fast/slow phase); (3) dampens with convergence — this is the diagnostic hallmark of infantile nystagmus syndrome (INS), also called congenital nystagmus. Patients often adopt a head turn to bring gaze to their null point where the nystagmus is least intense. The convergence dampening explains why some patients read with the book held very close — the convergence demand reduces the nystagmus. Acquired pendular nystagmus has a known adult onset (MS, brainstem lesion) and does not dampen with convergence. Jerk nystagmus has fast/slow phases. Gaze-evoked only appears at eccentric gaze, not in primary position.
k: The key features: (1) present since birth — congenital onset; (2) pendular in primary position (equal…

q: During binocular rivalry testing, a patient views a vertical grating with one eye and a horizontal grating with the other. The expected percept is:
o: Permanent suppression of the non-dominant eye's grating
w: Binocular rivalry occurs when the two eyes are shown incompatible images that cannot be fused. The brain cannot fuse a vertical and horizontal grating into a single stable percept. Instead, it alternates — one eye's image is perceived for a few seconds, then the other takes over, back and forth. This alternating suppression is the defining feature of binocular rivalry. It is NOT the same as permanent suppression (which occurs in constant strabismus with deep amblyopia). It is also not fusion because the stimuli are too dissimilar for the disparity detectors to merge them.
k: Binocular rivalry occurs when the two eyes are shown incompatible images that cannot be fused.

q: A patient with right amblyopia (VA 20/200, strabismic) undergoes stereopsis testing. The expected finding on Randot stereotest is:
o: Stereo unaffected because the left eye is normal
w: Strabismic amblyopia involves suppression of the amblyopic eye and disruption of binocular cortical neurons. Stereopsis requires both eyes to contribute corresponding images within Panum's fusional area simultaneously. In strabismus with deep suppression, the amblyopic eye's image is actively inhibited — binocular neurons are not stimulated normally. Without binocular cortical input, stereopsis cannot be generated regardless of monocular acuity in the dominant eye. Residual coarse stereopsis might persist with very mild or intermittent strabismus, but with deep constant amblyopia (20/200), stereopsis is essentially absent.
k: Strabismic amblyopia involves suppression of the amblyopic eye and disruption of binocular cortical neurons.

q: A 7-year-old has 20/20 acuity OU, no strabismus, and normal binocular vision. The teacher reports he cannot find objects on a busy worksheet and frequently loses his place while reading. Which assessment would best evaluate the most likely deficit?
w: The described difficulties — finding objects on a busy page (figure-ground), losing place while reading (visual sequential memory and figure-ground) — point to visual perceptual skills deficits. With normal acuity and binocular vision ruling out optical and alignment problems, the deficit is at the VPS level. TVPS-3 is the gold standard for assessing all 7 non-motor VPS subtopics in children ages 4–18. It requires only pointing, so motor output doesn't confound the results. Beery VMI would test visual-motor integration (copying shapes) — appropriate if handwriting or drawing difficulties were the chief complaint. VF testing and color testing are not relevant here.
k: The described difficulties — finding objects on a busy page (figure-ground), losing place while reading…

q: A 6-year-old scores poorly on the Beery VMI. Before concluding she has a visual perceptual deficit, which assessment should be done first to separate perception from motor output?
w: Beery VMI requires copying geometric shapes — a visual-motor task. A low score could reflect poor visual perception OR poor fine motor skills (shaky hands, poor pencil grip). TVPS-3 is the appropriate next step because it tests all 7 VPS subtopics with pointing responses only — no motor output is required. If TVPS-3 is normal, the Beery VMI deficit is motor (refer to OT for motor base work). If TVPS-3 is also poor, the deficit is perceptual (vision therapy targeting VPS). DTVP-2 also separates motor from non-motor but tests ages 4–10 and is less specific than TVPS-3 for identifying the perceptual subtype. Visual fields and acuity testing (A, D) do not assess VPS.
k: Beery VMI requires copying geometric shapes — a visual-motor task.

q: The foveal cone density is:
w: Foveal cone density: ~150,000/mm². Highest acuity zone

q: The Y-axis (AP) length is:
w: Y-axis (AP) length: 24 mm. Longest — full eyeball depth

q: The X and Z axes is:
w: X and Z axes: 22 mm each. Equal; shorter than AP
k: X and Z axes = 22 mm each

q: The center of rotation is:
w: Center of rotation: 13.5 mm behind cornea. All axes pass through here
k: Center of rotation = 13.5 mm behind cornea

q: The adult IPD average is:
w: Adult IPD average: 62.5 mm. Used in spectacle dispensing

q: The normal pupil range is:
w: Normal pupil range: 2–5 mm (physiologic 3–4 mm). Miotic ≤1.5 mm; mydriatic >5 mm

q: The F₂ (secondary focal point) is:
o: ~17 mm in front of the cornea
w: F₂ (secondary focal point): ~24 mm behind cornea. On retina in emmetropia
k: F₂ (secondary focal point) = ~24 mm behind cornea

q: Which term describes bright-light cone-mediated vision; peak sensitivity 555nm?
w: Photopic: Greek photos — light. Bright-light cone-mediated vision; peak sensitivity 555nm
k: Photopic = Bright-light cone-mediated vision; peak sensitivity 555nm

q: Which term describes the shift of peak spectral sensitivity from 555nm (photopic) to 507nm (scotopic) as light decreases?
w: Purkinje shift: Named after Jan E. Purkinje. The shift of peak spectral sensitivity from 555nm (photopic) to 507nm (scotopic) as light decreases
k: Purkinje shift = The shift of peak spectral sensitivity from 555nm (photopic) to 507nm (scotopic) as light dec

q: Which term describes the inflection point on the dark adaptation curve (~7min) where rod sensitivity takes over from cone sensitivity?
w: Kohlrausch kink: Named after Arnt Kohlrausch. The inflection point on the dark adaptation curve (~7min) where rod sensitivity takes over from cone sensitivity
k: Kohlrausch kink = The inflection point on the dark adaptation curve (~7min) where rod sensitivity takes over f

q: Which term describes the rod photopigment; bleaches on light exposure; regenerates in dark (requires Vit A)?
w: Rhodopsin: Greek rhodon — rose + opsis — sight. The rod photopigment; bleaches on light exposure; regenerates in dark (requires Vit A)
k: Rhodopsin = The rod photopigment; bleaches on light exposure; regenerates in dark (requires Vit A)

q: Which term describes red-sensitive cone pigment; peak ~750nm (L-cones)?
w: Erythrolabe: Greek erythros — red + labein — to take. Red-sensitive cone pigment; peak ~750nm (L-cones)

q: Which term describes green-sensitive cone pigment; peak ~535nm (M-cones)?
w: Chlorolabe: Greek khloros — green. Green-sensitive cone pigment; peak ~535nm (M-cones)

q: Which term describes blue-sensitive cone pigment; peak ~440nm (S-cones)?
w: Cyanolabe: Greek kyanos — blue. Blue-sensitive cone pigment; peak ~440nm (S-cones)

q: Which term describes point through which rays pass undeviated; angle alpha measured here?
w: Nodal point: Latin nodus — knot. Point through which rays pass undeviated; angle alpha measured here
k: Nodal point = Point through which rays pass undeviated; angle alpha measured here

q: Which term describes the 6 key points that define the optical system of the eye?
w: Cardinal points: Latin cardinalis — principal. The 6 key points that define the optical system of the eye
k: Cardinal points = The 6 key points that define the optical system of the eye

q: Which term describes brodmann area 18; secondary visual processing?
w: Parastriate cortex: Para — beside. Brodmann area 18; secondary visual processing
k: Parastriate cortex = Brodmann area 18; secondary visual processing

q: Which term describes brodmann area 19; higher processing (motion, form, object recognition)?
w: Peristriate cortex: Peri — around. Brodmann area 19; higher processing (motion, form, object recognition)
k: Peristriate cortex = Brodmann area 19; higher processing (motion, form, object recognition)

q: Which term describes "What" pathway: temporal lobe; object recognition?
w: Ventral stream: Latin venter — belly (flows "below"). "What" pathway: temporal lobe; object recognition
k: Ventral stream = "What" pathway: temporal lobe; object recognition

q: Which term describes "Where" pathway: parietal lobe; spatial location, motion?
w: Dorsal stream: Latin dorsum — back (flows "above"). "Where" pathway: parietal lobe; spatial location, motion
k: Dorsal stream = "Where" pathway: parietal lobe; spatial location, motion

q: Which term describes the retina has two systems: scotopic (rods) + photopic (cones)?
w: Duplicity theory: Latin duplicitas — doubleness. The retina has two systems: scotopic (rods) + photopic (cones)
k: Duplicity theory = The retina has two systems: scotopic (rods) + photopic (cones)

q: The VF inferior extent is:
w: VF inferior extent: 75°. Less obstruction below

q: The VF temporal extent is:
w: VF temporal extent: 100°. Widest extent

q: The VF nasal extent is:
w: VF nasal extent: 60°. Nose limits nasal field

q: The binocular overlap is:
w: Binocular overlap: 120° central. Both eyes see this zone simultaneously

q: The monocular crescent is:
w: Monocular crescent: 30° each side. Temporal retina only; no binocular input

q: The amsler grid coverage is:
w: Amsler grid coverage: Central 10°. At 28–30 cm distance

q: The amsler grid 1 square is:
w: Amsler grid 1 square: 1° of visual field. For documenting defect size
k: Amsler grid 1 square = 1° of visual field

q: Which term describes a localized area of reduced or absent vision within the visual field?
w: Scotoma: Greek skotos — darkness. A localized area of reduced or absent vision within the visual field
k: Scotoma = A localized area of reduced or absent vision within the visual field

q: Which term describes loss of a quarter of the visual field?
w: Quadrantanopia: Latin quadrans — quarter. Loss of a quarter of the visual field
k: Quadrantanopia = Loss of a quarter of the visual field

q: Which term describes systematic mapping of the visual field?
w: Perimetry: Greek peri — around + metron — measure. Systematic mapping of the visual field
k: Perimetry = Systematic mapping of the visual field

q: Which term describes measurement unit for Goldmann bowl background and stimuli?
w: Apostilb: Unit of luminance. Measurement unit for Goldmann bowl background and stimuli
k: Apostilb = Measurement unit for Goldmann bowl background and stimuli

q: Which term describes three cone types present but one has shifted peak absorption?
w: Anomalous trichromacy: Greek anomalos — irregular. Three cone types present but one has shifted peak absorption
k: Anomalous trichromacy = Three cone types present but one has shifted peak absorption

q: Which term describes only two functioning cone types; one type absent?
w: Dichromacy: Greek di — two. Only two functioning cone types; one type absent
k: Dichromacy = Only two functioning cone types; one type absent

q: Which term describes only one cone type (or rods only); complete color blindness?
w: Monochromacy: Greek mono — one. Only one cone type (or rods only); complete color blindness
k: Monochromacy = Only one cone type (or rods only); complete color blindness

q: Which term describes blending of two ocular images into one percept?
w: Fusion: Latin fundere — to pour together. Blending of two ocular images into one percept
k: Fusion = Blending of two ocular images into one percept

q: Which term describes latent tendency of the eyes to deviate; only present under dissociation?
w: Phoria: Greek phora — bearing/tendency. Latent tendency of the eyes to deviate; only present under dissociation
k: Phoria = Latent tendency of the eyes to deviate; only present under dissociation

q: Which term describes manifest (constant) deviation; always present?
w: Tropia: Greek tropos — turn. Manifest (constant) deviation; always present

q: Which term describes ideal alignment with no deviation tendency?
w: Orthophoria: Greek orthos — straight. Ideal alignment with no deviation tendency
k: Orthophoria = Ideal alignment with no deviation tendency

q: Which term describes both foveas have the same visual direction?
w: NRC: Normal Retinal Correspondence. Both foveas have the same visual direction
k: NRC = Both foveas have the same visual direction

q: Which term describes fovea of one eye + extrafoveal area of other eye share a common visual direction?
w: ARC: Anomalous Retinal Correspondence. Fovea of one eye + extrafoveal area of other eye share a common visual direction
k: ARC = Fovea of one eye + extrafoveal area of other eye share a common visual direction

q: Which term describes zone around the horopter where fusion (not diplopia) occurs despite non-corresponding stimulation?
w: Panum's area: Named after Peter Ludvig Panum. Zone around the horopter where fusion (not diplopia) occurs despite non-corresponding stimulation
k: Panum's area = Zone around the horopter where fusion (not diplopia) occurs despite non-corresponding stimulati

q: Which term describes active inhibition of one eye's visual signal by the brain?
w: Suppression: Latin suppressio — pressing down. Active inhibition of one eye's visual signal by the brain
k: Suppression = Active inhibition of one eye's visual signal by the brain

q: Which term describes reduced visual acuity in one eye not attributable to structural pathology; "lazy eye"?
w: Amblyopia: Greek amblys — dull + ops — eye. Reduced visual acuity in one eye not attributable to structural pathology; "lazy eye"
k: Amblyopia = Reduced visual acuity in one eye not attributable to structural pathology; "lazy eye"

q: Which term describes double vision from non-corresponding retinal stimulation?
w: Diplopia: Greek diploos — double. Double vision from non-corresponding retinal stimulation
k: Diplopia = Double vision from non-corresponding retinal stimulation

q: Scheerer's blue field phenomenon, seen as tiny bright dots on squiggly paths, is caused by:
o: White blood cells moving in retinal capillaries
w: Leukocytes pass through perifoveal capillaries while red cells absorb blue light, so the WBCs appear as moving bright dots against a blue field. It is a normal entoptic finding.
k: Blue field (Scheerer's) = WBCs in retinal capillaries.

q: Purkinje figures are entoptic images of the:
w: Oblique illumination casts the shadows of the retinal vessels onto the photoreceptors, making the patient's own vascular tree visible. Normally the image is stabilised and suppressed.
k: Purkinje figures = shadows of one's own retinal vessels.

q: Moore's lightning streaks are associated with:
w: Moore's lightning streaks are flashes from vitreous traction on the retina, often stress-related and usually benign, but they overlap with the flashes of a retinal tear and warrant a dilated look.

q: A phosphene is the perception of light produced by:
w: A phosphene is light perceived without any light entering the eye, from mechanical or electrical stimulation of the retinal ganglion cells. Pressing on a closed lid produces one.
k: Phosphene = light perceived with no light entering.

q: The PHYSIOLOGIC colored halo around a point light source is produced by:
w: The radial arrangement of lens fibers diffracts light into a colored halo, which is normal. A PATHOLOGIC halo comes from corneal edema in raised IOP or radiation keratopathy, and that one matters.
k: Physiologic halo = lens fiber diffraction; pathologic = corneal edema.
